chunks.title: ISEN 617: Quantitative Models for Supply Chain Management
chunks.content:
ISEN 617: Quantitative Models for Supply Chain Management
Spring Semester 2026 – TR 12:45 – 2:00PM, Zac 266

chunks.title: Catalog Description
chunks.content: Concepts, complexities, and models pertaining to supply chain management and relation of those to recent practical initiatives; includes channel coordination models, supply chain contracting, and vendor-managed, inventory models.

chunks.title: Course Description
chunks.content: This course covers the essential elements of Supply Chain Management, which consist of controlling and coordinating activities such as order processing, purchasing, material storage, production planning, transportation, facility location, contracting, and setting customer service standards. We will learn how these activities are successfully modeled, planned, and controlled in manufacturing and service industries. The decisions and strategies regarding transportation and inventory in a supply chain, which are the most important parts of supply chain operations for a firm, are highlighted. During the course, we will discuss several case studies of manufacturing and service firms in the US and abroad with a focus on their supply chain activities.

chunks.title: Prerequisites
chunks.content: Students should have had prior coursework in mathematical programming, basic stochastic processes and probability models.

chunks.title: Course Objectives
chunks.content: Students who have taken this course will be able to model operations processes involving inventory and transportation costs in multistage systems with a goal of achieving minimum relevant system costs or maximum relevant system profits while achieving service level goals. Students will understand operations tradeoffs and conflicting incentives at different stages in a supply chain, and will develop the ability to apply a set of tools and policies needed to coordinate operations at different supply chain stages.

chunks.title: Required Text
chunks.content:
Required Text: Fundamentals of Supply Chain Theory, 2nd ed., L.V. Snyder and Z.-J.M. Shen, ISBN 978-1-119-02484-
2. https://ebookcentral.proquest.com/lib/tamucs/detail.action?docID=5808411

chunks.title: Quizzes will be in class on the following dates
Course Outline
chunks.content:
| Topic | Chapter(s) |
|---|---|
| Introduction | 1 |
| Forecasting: Classical Methods and Forecast Accuracy | 2 |
| Demand Modeling | 2 |
| Deterministic Inventory Models: The Economic Order Quantity | 3 |
| Deterministic Inventory Models: Power-of-Two Policies, Quantity Discounts, Backorders, and Economic Production Quantity | 3 |
| Deterministic Inventory Models: Dynamic, Deterministic Demand | 3 |
| Stochastic Inventory Models: Periodic Review with Zero Fixed Costs | 4 |
| Stochastic Inventory Models: Periodic Review with Nonzero Fixed Costs | 4 |
| Stochastic Inventory Models: Continuous Review | 5 |
| Multiechelon Inventory Models: Introduction | 6 |
| Multiechelon Inventory Models: Series Systems with Stochastic Demand | 6 |
| Multiechelon Inventory Models: Multistage EOQ Type Models | 6 |
| Multiechelon Inventory Models: Guaranteed Service Models | 6 |
| Pooling and Flexibility | 7 |
| Facility Location Models | 8 |
| Supply Uncertainty | 9 |
| The Traveling Salesman Problem | 10 |
| The Vehicle Routing Problem | 11 |
| Integrated Supply Chain Models | 12 |
| The Bullwhip Effect | 13 |
| Supply Chain Contracts | 14 |

chunks.title: Requirements
chunks.content:
Homework: homework assignments will be distributed via Canvas.
Tests: Four quizzes (dates listed above).

chunks.title: Remarks
chunks.content: 1. Students may work together on homework, but identical submissions will receive zero grade.

chunks.title: Spring 2025 Course Calendar
chunks.content:
We will conform to the University Academic Calendar:
http://registrar.tamu.edu/Catalogs,-Policies-Procedures/Academic-Calendar
Consideration for University Authorized Absences will be made (see https://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07/).

chunks.title: Attendance
chunks.content: Attendance will not be taken during class and is not required except on days where an exam will be given. Lectures will support and supplement material in the course text and all exam dates and homework assignments will be announced in class only. You are responsible for and expected to know all material covered both in the course text and in lectures. Absence during an exam is only allowable with a University approved excuse. Missing an exam without approved absence will result in a grade of zero for the exam with no option to retake.

chunks.title: Final grade policy
chunks.content: I have never given extra projects or individual deals to improve grades. I do my best throughout the semester to help you learn the material. As we approach the end of a semester, and all in the course is said and done, you will have earned what you have earned. Therefore, please do not ask for help with your grade through some special arrangement; providing such help is unfair to everyone else who is not asking for or getting a special arrangement. I will not engage in such conversations and will ask you to leave. Please save me and yourself from a very uncomfortable conversation, because I will submit the grade you earned in the course.

chunks.title: Exam Conduct
chunks.content: Exams will be closed-book and a two-sided sheet of notes will be permitted, as will a calculator. Absolutely no cell phones or other electronic devices may be present during an exam (unless approved by the instructor). If there is any indication that an individual is compromising the integrity of an examination, the individual will be asked to leave and appropriate University protocol will be followed.

warnings: Course calendar year mismatch: Spring 2025 mentioned, but course is Spring 2026.
Homework assignments distribution method mentioned (Canvas), but no specific due dates or schedule.
